instruction: How should I structure a Rust library crate's module hierarchy? Show best practices for pub(crate), re-exports, and creating a clean public API.
output:
<think>
Rust module design is critical for API ergonomics. The key principles:

1. Internal organization != public API. Use pub(crate) extensively for internal helpers that shouldn't leak to users.
2. Re-export from lib.rs to create a flat, intuitive public API even if internal structure is deeply nested.
3. The prelude pattern: a convenience module that users can glob-import for the most common types.

Common mistakes I see:
- Making everything `pub` because the compiler complains about visibility
- Deep module nesting that forces `use mycrate::network::http::client::HttpClient`
- Not using `pub use` re-exports, forcing users to know internal structure
- Putting too much in lib.rs instead of delegating to modules

I'll design a hypothetical networking library to demonstrate:
- lib.rs as the public API surface (re-exports only)
- Modules organized by concern internally
- pub(crate) for internal types
- A prelude module for convenience
- Feature-gated modules for optional functionality
</think>

```rust
// === Project structure ===
// src/
//   lib.rs          <- Public API surface (re-exports)
//   client.rs       <- HTTP client
//   request.rs      <- Request builder
//   response.rs     <- Response types
//   error.rs        <- Error types
//   middleware/
//     mod.rs        <- Middleware trait + re-exports
//     retry.rs      <- Retry middleware
//     logging.rs    <- Logging middleware
//   internal/
//     mod.rs        <- Internal utilities (pub(crate) only)
//     pool.rs       <- Connection pool
//   prelude.rs      <- Convenience glob import
//   tls.rs          <- Feature-gated TLS support

// === src/lib.rs — Clean public API via re-exports ===

//! # netkit
//! A composable HTTP client library.
//!
//! ```
//! use netkit::prelude::*;
//! ```

mod client;
mod error;
mod request;
mod response;
pub mod middleware; // Users need to implement Middleware trait
mod internal;       // Never pub — all pub(crate) inside

// Feature-gated module
#[cfg(feature = "tls")]
mod tls;

// Re-export public API — users see flat structure
pub use client::Client;
pub use error::{Error, Result};
pub use request::{Request, RequestBuilder};
pub use response::Response;

#[cfg(feature = "tls")]
pub use tls::TlsConfig;

/// Prelude for convenient glob imports
pub mod prelude {
    pub use crate::{
        Client, Error, Request, RequestBuilder, Response, Result,
    };
    pub use crate::middleware::Middleware;
    #[cfg(feature = "tls")]
    pub use crate::TlsConfig;
}

// === src/error.rs ===
use std::fmt;

/// All errors this crate can produce.
#[derive(Debug)]
pub enum Error {
    /// HTTP-level error with status code
    Http(u16, String),
    /// Network connectivity error
    Connection(String),
    /// Request timeout
    Timeout(std::time::Duration),
    /// TLS handshake failure
    #[cfg(feature = "tls")]
    Tls(String),
}

pub type Result<T> = std::result::Result<T, Error>;

impl fmt::Display for Error {
    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {
        match self {
            Error::Http(code, msg) => write!(f, "HTTP {code}: {msg}"),
            Error::Connection(msg) => write!(f, "connection error: {msg}"),
            Error::Timeout(dur) => write!(f, "timeout after {dur:?}"),
            #[cfg(feature = "tls")]
            Error::Tls(msg) => write!(f, "TLS error: {msg}"),
        }
    }
}

impl std::error::Error for Error {}

// === src/internal/mod.rs — pub(crate) internals ===
pub(crate) mod pool;

// === src/internal/pool.rs — Never visible outside crate ===
use std::collections::VecDeque;

pub(crate) struct ConnectionPool {
    connections: VecDeque<Connection>,
    max_size: usize,
}

pub(crate) struct Connection {
    pub(crate) id: u64,
    pub(crate) alive: bool,
}

impl ConnectionPool {
    pub(crate) fn new(max_size: usize) -> Self {
        Self {
            connections: VecDeque::with_capacity(max_size),
            max_size,
        }
    }

    pub(crate) fn acquire(&mut self) -> Option<Connection> {
        self.connections.pop_front()
    }

    pub(crate) fn release(&mut self, conn: Connection) {
        if self.connections.len() < self.max_size {
            self.connections.push_back(conn);
        }
    }
}

// === src/middleware/mod.rs ===
mod retry;
mod logging;

pub use retry::RetryMiddleware;
pub use logging::LoggingMiddleware;

use crate::{Request, Response, Result};

/// Middleware trait — users implement this for custom middleware.
pub trait Middleware: Send + Sync {
    fn handle(&self, req: Request, next: &dyn Fn(Request) -> Result<Response>)
        -> Result<Response>;
}

// === src/client.rs ===
use crate::internal::pool::ConnectionPool;
use crate::middleware::Middleware;
use crate::{Request, Response, Result};

pub struct Client {
    pool: ConnectionPool,                   // pub(crate) type — hidden from users
    middlewares: Vec<Box<dyn Middleware>>,
}

impl Client {
    pub fn new() -> Self {
        Self {
            pool: ConnectionPool::new(10),
            middlewares: Vec::new(),
        }
    }

    pub fn with_middleware(mut self, mw: impl Middleware + 'static) -> Self {
        self.middlewares.push(Box::new(mw));
        self
    }

    pub fn send(&mut self, req: Request) -> Result<Response> {
        let _conn = self.pool.acquire();
        // ... execute request through middleware chain
        todo!()
    }
}
```

instruction: Explain the Rust prelude pattern for library crates. When should I create one, what goes in it, and what are the pitfalls?
output:
<think>
The prelude pattern in Rust is a convenience module that users can glob-import (`use mycrate::prelude::*`) to get all commonly-used types without individual imports.

The standard library does this (`std::prelude`) and many popular crates do too (bevy, tokio, etc.).

When to create a prelude:
- When users typically need 5+ imports from your crate
- When there's a clear "default set" of types everyone uses
- When the crate has a discoverability problem (too many modules)

What goes in:
- Core traits users need to implement or call methods on
- Main types (Client, Server, Request, Response)
- Common type aliases and Result types
- Derive macros

What does NOT go in:
- Everything — preludes should be curated, not exhaustive
- Internal types or rarely-used utilities
- Types that might conflict with std or other popular crates

Pitfalls:
- Name collisions: if your prelude exports `Error` or `Result`, it shadows std
- Over-stuffing: a prelude with 50 types defeats the purpose
- Users who don't want glob imports still need per-type imports to work
- Breaking changes: adding to prelude can break downstream glob imports

I should show a well-designed prelude vs a poorly-designed one.
</think>

```rust
// === Good prelude — curated, minimal, documented ===

// src/prelude.rs
//! Convenience re-exports for common usage.
//!
//! ```
//! use myorm::prelude::*;
//! ```
//!
//! This imports the core traits and types needed for most operations.
//! For advanced usage, import specific modules directly.

// Core traits — users need these for method resolution
pub use crate::query::{Queryable, Insertable, Filterable};
pub use crate::model::Model;
pub use crate::connection::Connect;

// Main types
pub use crate::Connection;
pub use crate::Schema;
pub use crate::Migration;

// Result alias (named to avoid shadowing std::result::Result)
pub use crate::error::DbResult;

// Derive macros (re-exported from proc-macro crate)
pub use myorm_derive::{Model, Queryable};


// === Bad prelude — don't do this ===

pub mod bad_prelude {
    // PROBLEM: Re-exports `Result` and `Error` — shadows std
    // pub use crate::error::{Result, Error};

    // PROBLEM: Exports internal types users rarely need
    // pub use crate::internal::PoolConfig;
    // pub use crate::internal::ConnectionState;

    // PROBLEM: Too many types — defeats discoverability
    // pub use crate::*;  // Never glob re-export everything
}


// === Real-world example: a game engine crate ===

// src/lib.rs
mod ecs;
mod math;
mod renderer;
mod input;
mod audio;
mod asset;

// Individual re-exports for users who want precision
pub use ecs::World;
pub use math::{Vec2, Vec3, Mat4, Quat};
pub use renderer::{Renderer, Sprite, Camera};
pub use input::{Input, Key, MouseButton};

pub mod prelude {
    //! Glob-import for rapid prototyping.
    //!
    //! Includes everything most games need. For production code,
    //! prefer explicit imports to avoid name collisions.

    // ECS
    pub use crate::ecs::{World, Entity, Component, System, Query};

    // Math (these are used everywhere, safe to glob)
    pub use crate::math::{Vec2, Vec3, Mat4, Quat};

    // Core rendering
    pub use crate::renderer::{Sprite, Camera, Color};

    // Input
    pub use crate::input::{Key, MouseButton};

    // Traits (needed for method resolution)
    pub use crate::ecs::{ComponentExt, SystemExt};
    pub use crate::renderer::Drawable;

    // NOT included: Renderer, AudioEngine, AssetLoader
    // (these are created once in setup, explicit import is fine)
}

// === Usage in downstream code ===

/*
// Quick prototyping:
use mygame::prelude::*;

// Production:
use mygame::ecs::{World, Entity, Component};
use mygame::math::Vec3;
use mygame::renderer::Sprite;
*/

// === Versioning consideration ===
// Adding a new item to prelude is a MINOR version bump (new API surface).
// Removing an item from prelude is a MAJOR version bump (breaking change).
// This is because `use mycrate::prelude::*` makes all exports part of
// the user's namespace — adding an export can cause name collisions in
// downstream code that already has that name in scope.
```

instruction: Show how to use Rust feature flags to gate parts of a library API. Include Cargo.toml configuration, conditional compilation, and how to test feature combinations.
output:
<think>
Feature flags in Rust let crates offer optional functionality without forcing all users to compile everything. This is crucial for:

1. Optional dependencies (e.g., serde support, TLS backends)
2. Reducing compile times for users who don't need everything
3. Platform-specific code
4. Unstable/experimental APIs

Key design principles:
- Features should be additive (enabling a feature should never remove API)
- Default features for the common case
- Feature names should be descriptive
- Document which features enable what
- Test all feature combinations in CI

I need to cover:
- Cargo.toml feature definition with optional deps
- #[cfg(feature = "...")] on modules, functions, impl blocks, derives
- Feature-gated trait implementations
- Testing different feature combos with cargo test --features
- CI matrix for feature combinations
</think>

```toml
# Cargo.toml
[package]
name = "datastore"
version = "0.3.0"
edition = "2021"

[features]
# Default features — what users get without specifying anything
default = ["json"]

# Serialization backends
json = ["dep:serde_json", "dep:serde"]
msgpack = ["dep:rmp-serde", "dep:serde"]

# Optional TLS support
tls = ["dep:rustls", "dep:tokio-rustls"]

# Async runtime support
async-tokio = ["dep:tokio"]
async-async-std = ["dep:async-std"]

# Tracing/observability
tracing = ["dep:tracing"]

# Unstable APIs (not covered by semver)
unstable = []

# Meta-feature: enable everything
full = ["json", "msgpack", "tls", "async-tokio", "tracing"]

[dependencies]
serde = { version = "1", features = ["derive"], optional = true }
serde_json = { version = "1", optional = true }
rmp-serde = { version = "1", optional = true }
rustls = { version = "0.23", optional = true }
tokio-rustls = { version = "0.26", optional = true }
tokio = { version = "1", features = ["full"], optional = true }
async-std = { version = "1", optional = true }
tracing = { version = "0.1", optional = true }

[dev-dependencies]
tokio = { version = "1", features = ["full"] }  # Always available in tests
```

```rust
// src/lib.rs

mod store;
mod error;

// Feature-gated modules
#[cfg(feature = "json")]
mod json_backend;

#[cfg(feature = "msgpack")]
mod msgpack_backend;

#[cfg(feature = "tls")]
pub mod tls;

#[cfg(feature = "async-tokio")]
mod async_impl;

// Public API — always available
pub use store::{Store, Record};
pub use error::{Error, Result};

// Conditional re-exports
#[cfg(feature = "json")]
pub use json_backend::JsonBackend;

#[cfg(feature = "msgpack")]
pub use msgpack_backend::MsgpackBackend;

#[cfg(feature = "async-tokio")]
pub use async_impl::AsyncStore;

// Unstable API
#[cfg(feature = "unstable")]
pub mod experimental {
    /// Batch operations — API may change without semver bump.
    pub fn batch_insert() {
        todo!()
    }
}

// src/store.rs
use crate::Result;

pub struct Record {
    pub key: String,
    pub value: Vec<u8>,
}

// Conditional derive — only add Serialize/Deserialize when serde is enabled
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
#[cfg_attr(feature = "serde", derive(serde::Serialize, serde::Deserialize))]
pub struct Store {
    path: String,
    records: Vec<Record>,
}

impl Store {
    pub fn new(path: &str) -> Self {
        Self { path: path.to_string(), records: Vec::new() }
    }

    pub fn insert(&mut self, key: String, value: Vec<u8>) -> Result<()> {
        self.records.push(Record { key, value });
        Ok(())
    }

    // Feature-gated method
    #[cfg(feature = "json")]
    pub fn to_json(&self) -> Result<String> {
        serde_json::to_string_pretty(&self)
            .map_err(|e| crate::Error::Serialization(e.to_string()))
    }

    // Feature-gated method
    #[cfg(feature = "tracing")]
    pub fn insert_traced(&mut self, key: String, value: Vec<u8>) -> Result<()> {
        tracing::info!(key = %key, bytes = value.len(), "inserting record");
        self.insert(key, value)
    }
}

// Feature-gated trait implementation
#[cfg(feature = "json")]
impl std::fmt::Display for Store {
    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut std::fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> std::fmt::Result {
        match self.to_json() {
            Ok(json) => write!(f, "{json}"),
            Err(e) => write!(f, "<serialization error: {e}>"),
        }
    }
}

// === Testing feature combinations ===

#[cfg(test)]
mod tests {
    use super::*;

    #[test]
    fn test_basic_insert() {
        // This test runs regardless of features
        let mut store = Store::new("/tmp/test");
        store.insert("key".into(), b"value".to_vec()).unwrap();
    }

    #[test]
    #[cfg(feature = "json")]
    fn test_json_serialization() {
        let store = Store::new("/tmp/test");
        let json = store.to_json().unwrap();
        assert!(json.contains("path"));
    }

    #[test]
    #[cfg(all(feature = "json", feature = "msgpack"))]
    fn test_roundtrip_json_to_msgpack() {
        // Only runs when both features are enabled
        let store = Store::new("/tmp/test");
        let json = store.to_json().unwrap();
        assert!(!json.is_empty());
    }
}

// === CI: test all feature combos (in .github/workflows/ci.yml) ===
// cargo test --no-default-features
// cargo test --features json
// cargo test --features msgpack
// cargo test --features "json,msgpack"
// cargo test --features tls
// cargo test --features full
// cargo test  # (default features)
```

instruction: How do I design a Rust crate with clean pub(crate) boundaries? I want internal helpers accessible across modules but hidden from external users.
output:
<think>
This is about the visibility system in Rust, which is more nuanced than most languages. The key visibility levels:

- `pub` — visible to everyone (external API)
- `pub(crate)` — visible within the crate, hidden from external users
- `pub(super)` — visible to parent module only
- `pub(in crate::some::path)` — visible to a specific ancestor module
- private (no modifier) — visible only within the current module

The common pattern:
1. Internal utility modules with `pub(crate)` items
2. Public-facing modules that use internal helpers but don't expose them
3. lib.rs that only re-exports the public API

Mistakes developers make:
- Using `pub` when they mean `pub(crate)` because "the compiler said it needed to be pub"
- Not realizing that `pub` in a private module is effectively `pub(crate)` — but this is fragile; if the module is later made public, everything leaks
- Putting helpers in the same module as public types, making it hard to audit the API surface

I'll create a practical example: a parser crate where the tokenizer is internal but the AST and parser are public.
</think>

```rust
// === src/lib.rs ===
// Only pub items here define the external API

mod tokenizer;    // internal — never pub mod
mod parser;
mod ast;
mod error;
mod helpers;      // internal utilities

// Public API: exactly what users can access
pub use ast::{Expr, Statement, Program};
pub use parser::Parser;
pub use error::{ParseError, ParseResult};

// Users CANNOT access:
// - tokenizer::Tokenizer (never re-exported)
// - helpers::* (never re-exported)
// - Any pub(crate) items


// === src/helpers.rs ===
// Utility functions used across modules but never exposed externally

/// Visible to all modules in this crate, but not external users.
pub(crate) fn normalize_whitespace(s: &str) -> String {
    s.split_whitespace().collect::<Vec<_>>().join(" ")
}

pub(crate) fn is_identifier_char(c: char) -> bool {
    c.is_alphanumeric() || c == '_'
}

/// Even more restricted: only visible to parent (lib.rs) and siblings
pub(super) fn debug_dump(msg: &str) {
    #[cfg(debug_assertions)]
    eprintln!("[DEBUG] {msg}");
}


// === src/tokenizer.rs ===
// Entirely internal module — not `pub mod` in lib.rs

use crate::helpers;

/// Token type — pub(crate) so parser can use it, but external users cannot
#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq)]
pub(crate) enum Token {
    Ident(String),
    Number(f64),
    LParen,
    RParen,
    Plus,
    Minus,
    Star,
    Slash,
    Eof,
}

pub(crate) struct Tokenizer {
    input: Vec<char>,
    pos: usize,
}

impl Tokenizer {
    pub(crate) fn new(source: &str) -> Self {
        Self {
            input: source.chars().collect(),
            pos: 0,
        }
    }

    pub(crate) fn tokenize(&mut self) -> Vec<Token> {
        let mut tokens = Vec::new();
        while self.pos < self.input.len() {
            match self.input[self.pos] {
                c if c.is_whitespace() => self.pos += 1,
                c if helpers::is_identifier_char(c) => {
                    tokens.push(self.read_ident());
                }
                '(' => { tokens.push(Token::LParen); self.pos += 1; }
                ')' => { tokens.push(Token::RParen); self.pos += 1; }
                '+' => { tokens.push(Token::Plus); self.pos += 1; }
                _ => self.pos += 1,
            }
        }
        tokens.push(Token::Eof);
        tokens
    }

    fn read_ident(&mut self) -> Token {
        let start = self.pos;
        while self.pos < self.input.len()
            && helpers::is_identifier_char(self.input[self.pos])
        {
            self.pos += 1;
        }
        Token::Ident(self.input[start..self.pos].iter().collect())
    }
}


// === src/ast.rs ===
// Public types — part of the external API

/// An expression node. Public — users pattern-match on this.
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub enum Expr {
    Literal(f64),
    Variable(String),
    BinaryOp {
        op: BinOp,
        left: Box<Expr>,
        right: Box<Expr>,
    },
    Call {
        name: String,
        args: Vec<Expr>,
    },
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
pub enum BinOp {
    Add, Sub, Mul, Div,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct Statement {
    pub expr: Expr,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct Program {
    pub statements: Vec<Statement>,
}

// Internal helper on a public type — method is pub(crate)
impl Expr {
    /// Count nodes — used by optimizer internally, not part of public API.
    pub(crate) fn node_count(&self) -> usize {
        match self {
            Expr::Literal(_) | Expr::Variable(_) => 1,
            Expr::BinaryOp { left, right, .. } => {
                1 + left.node_count() + right.node_count()
            }
            Expr::Call { args, .. } => {
                1 + args.iter().map(|a| a.node_count()).sum::<usize>()
            }
        }
    }
}


// === src/parser.rs ===
use crate::tokenizer::{Token, Tokenizer}; // Access pub(crate) types
use crate::ast::*;
use crate::error::ParseResult;

pub struct Parser {
    tokens: Vec<Token>,  // Token is pub(crate) — hidden from users
    pos: usize,
}

impl Parser {
    /// Public API — users create a parser from source.
    pub fn new(source: &str) -> Self {
        let mut tokenizer = Tokenizer::new(source);
        Self {
            tokens: tokenizer.tokenize(),
            pos: 0,
        }
    }

    /// Public API — parse into a Program.
    pub fn parse(&mut self) -> ParseResult<Program> {
        let mut statements = Vec::new();
        while !self.at_eof() {
            statements.push(self.parse_statement()?);
        }
        Ok(Program { statements })
    }

    // Private helpers — not even pub(crate)
    fn at_eof(&self) -> bool {
        matches!(self.tokens.get(self.pos), Some(Token::Eof) | None)
    }

    fn parse_statement(&mut self) -> ParseResult<Statement> {
        let expr = self.parse_expr()?;
        Ok(Statement { expr })
    }

    fn parse_expr(&mut self) -> ParseResult<Expr> {
        match &self.tokens[self.pos] {
            Token::Ident(name) => {
                let name = name.clone();
                self.pos += 1;
                Ok(Expr::Variable(name))
            }
            Token::Number(n) => {
                let n = *n;
                self.pos += 1;
                Ok(Expr::Literal(n))
            }
            _ => Err(crate::error::ParseError::Unexpected(
                format!("{:?}", self.tokens[self.pos])
            )),
        }
    }
}
```
